text: Hashtag Black Lives Matter.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: This is a consistent trend we often see on the news and especially social media.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: But do we honestly understand what it means or why we even say it?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I mean, some of us only say it when an unarmed black man is shot and killed by the police.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And another part of us has substituted Black Lives Matter with All Lives Matter because we just don't understand the reason behind it.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: but that's why I'm here today to explain to you the reasonings behind this movement and why we say Black Lives Matter.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: It all began in February 2012 when George Zimmerman, the captain of his neighborhood watch party, reported a suspicious person in his neighborhood.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: He is then instructed to remain in his vehicle and to wait until police arrive.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: But instead, Zimmerman disregards these instructions and begins to follow 17-year-old Trayvon Martin.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Moments later, neighbors report hearing a gunshot.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: The sound of that gunshot is what killed 17-year-old Trayvon Martin.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: In 2013, Zimmerman was acquitted in the murdering of Trayvon Martin because it was in self-defense.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Regardless, we watched a man get away with murdering a child.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And Trayvon isn't the only person who has a story like this.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: In 2014, Tamir Rice
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: was shot and killed by the police for playing with a BB gun.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: He was only 12 years old.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And just like Trayvon and just like Tamir, I am an African-American teenager.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: That could have been me, that could have been you, or anybody else that you may know.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: In 2017, Mapping Police Violence has collected data for all the police killings that has happened in that year.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Exactly 1,147 people were killed by the police in 2017.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: 25% of those were African Americans, although African Americans only make up 13% of this country's population.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And out of all those killings, only 1% of them were convicted in the murdering of all these people.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And because of that, people are becoming so angry.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Artists like Jay-Z and Beyonce and entertainers such as Colin Kaepernick are speaking out about these situations.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Colin Kaepernick's Take a Knee movement has shook this country, literally.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Most people believe that what he's doing is very disrespectful.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: But you also have to remember that we once believed that Rosa Parks, when she would not give up that seat, we also believe that that was very disrespectful.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And in sports, we're taught that when someone is hurt and injured, we take a knee.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: When there's a fallen soldier on the battlefield, we also take a knee.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: We are not trying to be disrespectful by this.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: It's all means we love our country.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: We just want to make things better.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So we're doing this by taking a stance.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Well, technically a sit, but I mean, you have to look at both point of views sometimes.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: We take a knee because this country is hurting, we are injured, because we know that racism is still in existence today.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And that is why we take a knee.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And a lot of times it isn't racism, it's just the fact that a lot of us don't know because we grow up in these communities where we don't see a lot of diversity or we just don't understand.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: For example, in Flint, Michigan, there's a public health crisis.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: There's been one since 2014.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: There has been a lead seepage into their water.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: They can't wash their hair, they can't take showers, and all of this because of environmental racism.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Yes, environmental racism is a thing.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: It's when you take a community, mostly a poor community,
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: or minority community and put all these pollutants and toxins into their community.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Or let's take it home, for instance.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Say Oakwood.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Oakwood is a beautiful community.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: We all can agree to that.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Oakwood is beautiful, right?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: The grass is so much greener on the other side.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: If you go down the street a little bit into Dayton,
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: You see trash on the ground.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: There's cigarette campaigns on every store.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: There's corner stores everywhere.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: In Oakwood, you wouldn't find that.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: This is environmental racism.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So I encourage everyone to just get informed about what's going on in our own communities.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: You may be wondering, like, how does Black Lives Matter concern me?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Well,
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: A few years ago, John Crawford was shot and killed in the Beaver Creek Walmart.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: That could have been you.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: That could have been me.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: You probably know John Crawford yourself.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: This is happening in our own backyard.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: We just need to become more aware about it.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And I know you may be wondering, well, all lives matter.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Yes, all lives matter.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Well, at least they're supposed to matter.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: But if all lives matter, women still wouldn't be marching for their rights.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Dreamers wouldn't have to fight for their right to be in this country.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And I wouldn't have to stand here and say, black lives matter.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I'm not here to change anyone's mind.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I'm just here to educate you and hope that you can take something home with you.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: In order for us to keep moving forward, we can't keep taking steps backwards.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Thank you.
speaker: SPEAKER_00
